name: Australia
summary: English-speaking with a points-based study->485->skilled-PR route; high cost, tougher 485 rules, and Evidence Level 3 scrutiny for Bangladeshis add friction.

job-market.summary: Strong demand for software/ICT engineers in major cities.
job-market.reasoning: Sydney and Melbourne host large tech sectors; ICT/software occupations sit on the skilled occupation lists used for migration.
evidence: Software/ICT occupations on skilled occupation lists
Tech demand concentrated in Sydney and Melbourne

visa-access.summary: No Australian visa office in Dhaka; Bangladesh at Evidence Level 3 (highest scrutiny).
visa-access.reasoning: Bangladesh was reclassified to Evidence Level 3 from January 2026, meaning the highest document scrutiny, financial verification, longer processing and elevated student-visa refusal rates under the Genuine Student requirement.
evidence: Bangladesh reclassified to Evidence Level 3 (Jan 2026)
Genuine Student requirement replaced GTE
Minimum living-cost proof AUD 29,710; high scrutiny of finances

citizenship.summary: Citizenship after 4 years lawful residence incl. 1 year as PR; dual citizenship allowed.
citizenship.reasoning: Applicants must have lived lawfully in Australia for 4 years before applying, including the last 12 months as a permanent resident, with limited absences; Australia has permitted dual citizenship since 2002.
evidence: 4 years lawful residence incl. last 12 months as PR
Absence limits: <12 months over 4 years, <90 days in final year
Dual citizenship permitted since 2002

post-study-work.summary: Subclass 485 ~2 years for coursework masters; age limit and extensions tightened.
post-study-work.reasoning: The Temporary Graduate (subclass 485) gives up to 2 years for a coursework master's (3 years for research master's/PhD); the general age limit dropped to 35 from July 2024 (50 for research masters/PhD) and the skill-shortage extension was discontinued.
evidence: Subclass 485 up to 2 years for coursework master's
Age limit 35 (general), 50 for research master's/PhD since July 2024
Skill-shortage 2-year extension discontinued

spouse-family.summary: Dependants can accompany; partner work rights vary by primary visa.
spouse-family.reasoning: Spouses can be included as dependants on student and skilled visas; partners of skilled/PR visa holders generally gain work rights, while student-visa partner work hours are capped.
evidence: Partner can be included as dependant on student and skilled visas
Partner work rights for skilled/PR primary holders
Student-visa partner work hours capped

msc-study.summary: Wide English MSc offering, but tuition and living costs are very high.
msc-study.reasoning: Strong universities with English-taught master's, but international tuition is high and required proof of funds is steep; students may work up to 48 hours per fortnight during term.
evidence: Broad English-taught master's programs
High international tuition and living-cost proof
Work up to 48 hours per fortnight during study

pr-pathway.summary: Points-based skilled PR (189/190) exists but increasingly competitive.
pr-pathway.reasoning: General Skilled Migration via SkillSelect (subclass 189/190/491) is a clear route, but the pass mark of 65 points is only nominal — competitive invitations for ICT now require roughly 85+ points, and the points test is being reformed.
evidence: GSM via SkillSelect (189/190/491)
Pass mark 65 but ICT invitations effectively need ~85+ points
2026 points-test reform underway

income-cost.summary: High tech salaries but very high housing costs in Sydney/Melbourne.
income-cost.reasoning: Software salaries are strong, but housing and living costs in Sydney and Melbourne are among the highest globally, limiting savings.
evidence: Strong tech salaries
Very high housing costs in Sydney and Melbourne

links.title: Australian Bureau of Statistics cost of living

healthcare.summary: Medicare for residents; students need Overseas Student Health Cover.
healthcare.reasoning: Medicare covers permanent residents and citizens; international students must hold Overseas Student Health Cover (OSHC) for the duration of their visa.
evidence: Medicare for PRs and citizens
OSHC mandatory for international students

culture-language.reasoning: English is the everyday and working language; a large multicultural population and established migrant communities make day-to-day integration easy for English speakers.
evidence: English working and everyday language
Highly multicultural population

safety-law.summary: Strong rule of law and generally low violent crime.
safety-law.reasoning: High rule-of-law ranking, stable legal system and generally safe cities, with anti-discrimination protections for migrants.
evidence: High WJP Rule of Law index ranking
Generally low violent crime

politics.summary: Stable democracy but volatile migration settings.
politics.reasoning: Stable institutions, but international-student and post-study-work policy has changed frequently since 2024 (caps, 485 tightening, evidence-level changes), adding uncertainty for newcomers.
evidence: Stable parliamentary democracy
Frequent 2024-2026 changes to student/485 and evidence-level settings

tax.summary: Progressive income tax with a high top marginal rate.
tax.reasoning: Resident income tax is progressive up to 45% plus the Medicare levy; non-resident rates start higher, reducing early take-home pay.
evidence: Top marginal resident rate 45% plus 2% Medicare levy
Higher non-resident tax rates

links.title: Australian Taxation Office individual tax rates

muslim-diaspora.summary: Established Muslim and South-Asian communities; halal widely available.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Sizeable Muslim and Bangladeshi communities in Sydney and Melbourne with mosques and halal food readily available; occasional anti-Muslim sentiment exists.
evidence: Muslim and Bangladeshi communities in Sydney/Melbourne
Mosques and halal food widely available

links.title: Australian Bureau of Statistics religion data
